dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding to a reported cardiac arrest at 18th & Belgrave in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 tonight?
My one-year-old baby boy was found unresponsive in his bed. We tried CPR but it didn't work.
He was found limp and not breathing, no pulse.
Let's check his responsiveness. Hey, can you hear us?
No response. Does he open his eyes to pain or loud stimulation?
Nope, no eye response. What about verbal response? Try louder or sternal rub?
No motor response to sternal rub or loud voice. He just stays limp.
Airway looks compromised. Where is he breathing? Looks like he's not breathing.
He's not breathing. Should we use a head tilt chin lift to open the airway for BVM?
Yes. His tongue might be obstructing or he's floppy.
I'm going to perform head tilt and chin lift now for the BVM.
Heard consistent opening and I'm preparing the pediatric BVM.
He is apneic. Do you see any chest rise or smell any odors?
No chest movement. No unusual odors. He's too young for drugs.
Put him on a pediatric mask. Let's begin ventilations using BVM with 25 liters of O2.
What about his pulse? Carotid and femoral check.
I checked but didn't feel any pulse earlier. Maybe double-check.
No pulse felt at carotid and femoral. No signs of bleeding. Pulse is zero.
What is the skin condition like?
Pale, warm, and dry. Trauma? Any punctures or wounds?
No punctures or significant trauma. I think he just looped his feeding tube around his neck somehow.
He's in asystole with agonal beats per 4-lead monitor.
No pulse - confirmed at 0.
No respirations at all - 0 breaths per minute.
No blood pressure reading. BP is 0/0 confirmed.
Can't get an accurate SpO2 reading with pediatric sensor - remains at 0.
Blood glucose is 129. Took a heel stick sample.
He is unresponsive and in cardiac arrest. No painful events communicated. Pain is 0.
What did you notice before calling 911? Any signs before he went unresponsive?
We found him unresponsive in his crib. He had a feeding tube looped around his neck.
Had the family already administered CPR in the home before we arrived?
Yes. We tried for a while, then couldn't get a response so we called 911.
Was he born prematurely or with complications at birth?
Yes, he's a twin and was born premature.
Can you tell me any medications he takes or has on a routine basis?
Not that I'm aware of.
Does he have a known allergy to any medications or food?
No known drug or food allergy.
Last time he had something to eat or drink?
I'm not sure. He had a feeding tube, but I'm not sure how long ago it was used.
We're treating for asystolic cardiac arrest per the national pediatric resuscitation protocol.
Let's place the patient on a pediatric 4-lead ECG.
I've got the pediatric pads placed on the patient. Ready to monitor.
Applying IO access at the left proximal tibial area using a Jamshidi needle after cleaning with betadine.
IO access secured. I've flushed with 10 mL normal saline and it's patent. Saline lock in place.
Let's prepare and administer 0.085 mg epinephrine in the IO line as per asystole protocol.
Double-check. Vial, dose, route - all good. Administering now. EtCO2 is approximately 8-9 mmHg.
Let's check his 4-lead again.
Still asystole with occasional agonal beats. His SpO2 remains at 0.
Check the IO again for proper patency.
No sign of infiltration. Flushed successfully.
Administer second 0.085 mg epinephrine via IO.
Using the same route and double-checked the vial once again.
What's the BVM response like after this second dose?
Adequate rise and fall witnessed. Chest squeeze looks consistent.
Still asystolic. I don't feel a pulse again. His GCS remains at 3 (E1, V1, M1).
Administer third 0.085 mg epinephrine via IO.
Want to go to VCU hospital for emergent stabilization and care.
That's the closest one and most appropriate for an infant in cardiac arrest.
Ready to load to the ambulance. Should we secure with safety?
Let's prepare and contact the ED for arrival.
Let me summarize the info for the hospital during transport.
We're leaving now with CPR continuing and monitoring in place.
Asystole, unresponsive, no response to multiple epinephrine rounds, total absence of spontaneous breaths.
ED has been contacted. Two firefighters will follow us.
Secure him to the stretcher. Keep the IO line in place during transport.
He's secured now. Ready to go. 4-lead, BVM, and EPI kit all set.
CPR resumes at a rate of 100-120 per minute with good depth - using two hands for compressions in infant CPR.
BVM ventilations at same time, two hands on mask per the 15:2 compression to ventilation ratio.
Good chest compression with consistent depth and release.
I need to suction the oral cavity to remove any secretions using the Yankauer.
Patient on board now. CPR resumes.
Two firefighters are joining us for infant resuscitation during ride.
All notes will be transferred to the emergency department quickly and handoff completed upon arrival.
Patient remains asystolic. No improvement noted so far.
VCU ED has been alerted. They'll expect immediate infant cardiac arrest.
Arms fastened, chest padded, legs flexed for safety.
Transport in progress. ED aware we're on the way with ETA shortly.
Protocol transfer notes on EKG strip and interventions documented.
Patient secured during transport with all equipment in place.
No signs of trauma. All interventions administered per standard protocol.
Given the presence of a competent airway and adequate chest rise, decision made to withhold advanced airway placement.
All per protocol 8-2. Epinephrine administered thrice. No pulse and spontaneous breaths remain zero.
Per protocol, epinephrine administered, IO established, CPR supervised and documented.
Patient arrived at VCU pediatric ED. All documentation has been transferred to the receiving team.
All field actions completed as per protocol 8-2.
